perturbation.original_text: 1.1. Affiliate shall mean, when used with reference to a party, any individual or entity directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by or under common control with such party. For purposes of this definition, "control" means the direct or indirect ownership of at least 50% of the outstanding voting securities of a party, or the right to control the policy decisions of such party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1. Affiliate shall mean, when used with reference to a party, any individual or entity directly or indirectly related to such party. For purposes of this definition, "control" means any influence over the policy decisions of such party, regardless of ownership percentage.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Affiliate' specifies a clear ownership threshold for control (50% of voting securities). The modified definition broadens the term 'control' to include 'any influence,' removing the specific ownership percentage. This introduces ambiguity and contradicts established legal interpretations of 'control' in corporate law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts the SEC's definition of 'control,' which often hinges on the power to direct the management and policies of a person, whether through ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise. Removing the concrete ownership percentage introduces uncertainty, potentially misclassifying entities as affiliates under securities regulations.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.12b-2 - Definitions. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 240.12b-2 Definitions. Unless the context otherwise requires, the following terms, when used in the rules contained in this regulation or in Regulation 13A or 15D or in the forms for statements and reports filed pursuant to sections 12, 13 or 15(d) of the act, shall have the respective meanings indicated in this rule: Accelerated filer and large accelerated filer —(1) Accelerated filer. The term accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $75 million or more, but less than $700 million, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 78m or 78o(d) ) for a period of at least twelve calendar months; and (iii) The issuer has filed at least one annual report pursuant to section 13(a) or 15(d) of the Act; and (iv) The issuer is not eligible to use the requirements for smaller reporting companies under the revenue test in paragraph (2) or (3)(iii)(B) of the “smaller reporting company” definition in this section , as applicable. (2) Large accelerated filer. The term large accelerated filer means an issuer after it first meets the following conditions as of the end of its fiscal year: (i) The issuer had an aggregate worldwide market value of the voting and non-voting common equity held by its non-affiliates of $700 million or more, as of the last business day of the issuer 's most recently completed second fiscal quarter; (ii) The issuer has been subject to the requirements of section 13(a) o
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts established legal definitions of 'control' by broadening it to 'any influence,' which deviates from the SEC's focus on the power to direct management and policies, often linked to ownership of voting securities. The original text's specific 50% ownership threshold is a legally recognized standard for establishing control, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity that could lead to misclassification of affiliates under securities regulations, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defining 'accelerated filer' and 'large accelerated filer' uses market value of voting and non-voting common equity held by non-affiliates as a measure. This implies a need for a concrete measure of control, which is undermined by the 'any influence' definition in the changed text. Thus the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition applies because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 1.7. Confidential Information shall mean all proprietary and confidential information of a party, including, without limitation, trade secrets, technical information, business information, sales information, customer and potential customer lists and identities, product sales plans, sublicense agreements, inventions, developments, discoveries, software, know-how, methods, techniques, formulae, data, processes and other trade secrets and proprietary ideas, whether or not protectable under patent, trademark, copyright or other areas of law, that the other party has access to or receives, but does not include information that (a) is or becomes publicly available through no fault of receiving party; (b) was already known to the receiving party at the time it was disclosed to the receiving party, as evidenced by written records of the receiving party; (c) is independently developed by employees of the receiving party who had no knowledge of or * Confidential Treatment Requested: material has been omitted and filed separately with the Commission. access to such information, as evidenced by written records of the receiving party; or (d) is received from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to the disclosing party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.7. Confidential Information shall mean information a party designates as such. It includes, but is not limited to, business information, customer lists, and product plans, but excludes information generally known. The receiving party's subjective belief about confidentiality shall govern.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition lists specific types of information considered confidential and provides clear exceptions. The modified definition relies solely on a party's subjective designation and significantly weakens the exclusions, especially concerning independently developed information. This broadens the scope of 'Confidential Information' and introduces considerable uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition contradicts the UTSA's requirements for trade secret protection, which include reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy and independent economic value from not being generally known. The UTSA requires an actual 'trade secret,' not just anything a party subjectively designates as confidential. The modification removes objective criteria for determining if the information is a trade secret, making it potentially unenforceable under trade secret law.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the principle of maintaining secrecy for trade secret protection, a core tenet often found in trade secret law (as suggested by the Law Explanation's reference to the UTSA, Uniform Trade Secrets Act). The modification, by relying on a party's subjective belief and broadly defining confidential information, weakens the requirement for 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.' While the provided scraped snippets are not directly helpful in confirming this, the Law Explanation does mention UTSA, strengthening the argument that the definition of 'Confidential Information' has been altered in a way that is not compliant with trade secret law. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction definition because the modified definition deviates from legally recognized definitions (or requirements) for what constitutes a trade secret. By making the scope so broad and subjective, it undermines enforceability of the clause under established trade secret law.

perturbation.original_text: 1.10. Intellectual Property shall mean any and all trade secrets, patents, copyrights, trademarks, URLs, trade dress, brand features, know-how and similar rights of any type under the laws of any applicable governmental authority, including, without limitation, all applications and registrations relating to any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.10. Intellectual Property shall mean any idea, concept, or expression a party believes is original to them. This includes, but is not limited to, mere thoughts or mental conceptions, regardless of legal protection.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition adheres to legally recognized categories of intellectual property, such as patents, copyrights, and trademarks, which are governed by specific laws. The modified definition drastically expands the scope to include any 'idea, concept, or expression' a party subjectively believes is original, regardless of whether it qualifies for legal protection or is even documented.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification directly contradicts copyright law, specifically 17 U.S.C. § 102(b), which states that copyright protection does not extend to any idea, procedure, process, system, method of operation, concept, principle, or discovery, regardless of the form in which it is described, explained, illustrated, or embodied. The modified definition attempts to include 'mere thoughts or mental conceptions,' which are explicitly excluded from copyright protection. This creates a contractual term that is fundamentally at odds with federal copyright law.

Subject matter of copyright: In general (a) Copyright protection subsists, in accordance with this title, in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression, now known or later developed, from which they can be perceived, reproduced, or otherwise communicated, either directly or with the aid of a machine or device. Works of authorship include the following categories: (1) literary works; (2) musical works, including
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 102 - Subject matter of copyright: In general | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 102 - Subject matter of copyright: In general U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Copyright protection subsists, in accordance with this title, in original works of authorship fixed in any tangible medium of expression, now known or later developed, from which they can be perceived, reproduced, or otherwise communicated, either directly or with the aid of a machine or device. Works of authorship include the following categories: (1) literary works ; (2) musical works, including any accompanying words; (3) dramatic works, including any accompanying music; (4) pantomimes and choreographic works; (5) pictorial, graphic, and sculptural works; (6) motion pictures and other audiovisual works ; (7) sound recordings ; and (8) architectural works . (b) In no case does copyright protection for an original work of authorship extend to any idea, procedure, process , system, method of operation, concept, principle, or discovery, regardless of the form in which it is described, explained, illustrated, or embodied in such work. ( Pub. L. 94–553, title I, § 101 , Oct. 19, 1976 , 90 Stat. 2544 ; Pub. L. 101–650, title VII, § 703 , Dec. 1, 1990 , 104 Stat. 5133 .) Historical and Revision Notes house report no. 94–1476 Original Works of Authorship. The two fundamental criteria of copyright protection—originality and fixation in tangible form are restated in the first sentence of this cornerstone provision. The phrase “original works or authorship,” which is purposely left undefined, is intended to incorporate without change the standard of originality established by the courts under the present copyright statute. This standard does not include requirements of novelty, ingenuity, or esthetic merit, and there is no intention to enlarge the standard of copyright protection to require them
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines "Intellectual Property" to include any 'idea, concept, or expression' a party believes is original, even 'mere thoughts or mental conceptions,' regardless of legal protection. This directly contradicts 17 U.S.C. § 102(b), as explained in the Law Explanation, which explicitly excludes ideas, processes, systems, methods of operation, concepts, principles, or discoveries from copyright protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 confirms this exclusion, stating: '(b) In no case does copyright protection for an original work of authorship extend to any idea, procedure, process , system, method of operation, concept, principle, or discovery...' The original definition adhered to legally recognized categories of intellectual property. The modified definition creates a contractual term that clashes with established copyright law, broadening the scope of intellectual property beyond what is legally protected and rendering the contract unenforceable in that aspect.
